subject: Statistics
title: Uninformative Priors
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Uninformative Priors.

hook.text: What if you truly know nothing? Can you express complete ignorance mathematically?

buildup.text: Uninformative priors try to let the data speak without influence. A flat prior gives equal weight to all values.

discovery.text: Jeffreys' prior is invariant to how you measure things — degrees or radians, the answer stays the same.

twist.text: True uninformativeness is impossible. Even a 'flat' prior assumes something — that extreme values are just as likely.

climax.text: In practice, weakly informative priors work best. They rule out absurdities without forcing specific beliefs.

punchline.text: You can't know nothing. But you can know very little, formally.

quiz.question: Why are truly uninformative priors problematic?
options: Even 'flat' priors make assumptions — like that extreme values are equally likely
They require too much computing power
They always produce the same posterior
